Gay rights: Oncale v. Sundowner Offshore Services, Inc.: The Supreme Court of the United States rules that federal laws banning on-the-job sexual harassment also apply when both parties are the same sex.

enAnchors.text: LGBT rights in the United States
Oncale v. Sundowner Offshore Services, Inc.

D0304.en: BBC bombing: A massive car bomb explodes in front of the BBC Television Centre in London, seriously injuring one person; the attack was attributed to the Real IRA.

Afghanistan: Seven American Special Operations Forces soldiers and 200 Al-Qaeda Fighters are killed as American forces attempt to infiltrate the Shah-i-Kot Valley on a low-flying helicopter reconnaissance mission.

The International Criminal Court (ICC) issues an arrest warrant for Sudanese President Omar Hassan al-Bashir for war crimes and crimes against humanity in Darfur. Al-Bashir is the first sitting head of state to be indicted by the ICC since its establishment in 2002.
A series of explosions is reported at a munitions dump in Brazzaville, the capital of the Republic of the Congo, killing at least 250 people.
At least 34 miners die in a suspected gas explosion at the Zasyadko coal mine in the rebel-held Donetsk region of Ukraine.

Former MI6 spy Sergei Skripal and his daughter are poisoned with a Novichok nerve agent in Salisbury, England, causing a diplomatic uproar that results in mass-expulsions of diplomats from all countries involved.
Nik Wallenda becomes the first person to walk over the Masaya Volcano in Nicaragua.[20]

D0305.en: Roman emperor Julian leaves Antioch with an army of 90,000 to attack the Sasanian Empire, in a campaign which would bring about his own death.[1]
Nasir Khusraw begins the seven-year Middle Eastern journey which he will later describe in his book Safarnama.
The Livonian Order is defeated in the Battle of Aizkraukle by the Grand Duchy of Lithuania.
King Henry VII of England issues letters patent to John Cabot and his sons, authorising them to explore unknown lands.
Nicolaus Copernicus's book On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres is added to the Index of Forbidden Books 73 years after it was first published.
Antonio de Ulloa, the first Spanish governor of Louisiana, arrives in New Orleans.

Boston Massacre: Five Americans, including Crispus Attucks, are fatally shot by British troops in an event that would contribute to the outbreak of the American Revolutionary War (also known as the American War of Independence) five years later.
Peninsular War: A French force under the command of Marshal Victor is routed while trying to prevent an Anglo-Spanish-Portuguese army from lifting the Siege of Cádiz in the Battle of Barrosa.[2]
First Anglo-Burmese War: The British officially declare war on Burma.
Roberto Cofresí, one of the last successful Caribbean pirates, is defeated in combat and captured by authorities.[3]
Samuel Colt established his first factory to produce recently patented production-model revolver, the .34-caliber "Paterson".[4]
The Britannia Bridge across the Menai Strait between the island of Anglesey and the mainland of Wales is opened.[5]
Parma, Tuscany, Modena and Romagna vote in referendums to join the Kingdom of Sardinia.
Mefistofele, an opera by Arrigo Boito, receives its premiere performance at La Scala.
George Westinghouse patents the air brake.
Moro Rebellion: United States Army troops bring overwhelming force against the native Moros in the First Battle of Bud Dajo, leaving only six survivors.
Italo-Turkish War: Italian forces are the first to use airships for military purposes, employing them for reconnaissance behind Turkish lines.
The British Raj: Gandhi–Irwin Pact is signed.
Adolf Hitler's Nazi Party receives 43.9% at the Reichstag elections, which allows the Nazis to later pass the Enabling Act and establish a dictatorship.
Spanish Civil War: The National Defence Council seizes control of the republican government in a coup d'etat, with the intention of negotiating an end to the war.[6]

Six high-ranking members of the Soviet politburo, including Joseph Stalin, sign an order for the execution of 25,700 Polish intelligentsia, including 14,700 Polish POWs, in what will become known as the Katyn massacre.

enAnchors.text: Politburo of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union

D0305.en: World War II: Japanese forces capture Batavia, capital of Dutch East Indies, which is left undefended after the withdrawal of the KNIL garrison and Australian Blackforce battalion to Buitenzorg and Bandung.

World War II: General strike and protest march in Athens against rumours of forced  mobilization of Greek workers for work in Germany,  resulting in clashes with the Axis occupation forces and collaborationist police. The decree is withdrawn on the next day.[7][8]
World War II: The Red Army begins the Uman–Botoșani offensive in the western Ukrainian SSR.
Cold War: Winston Churchill delivers his famous "Iron Curtain" speech at Westminster College, Missouri.
Joseph Stalin, the longest serving leader of the Soviet Union, dies at his Volynskoe dacha in Moscow after suffering a cerebral hemorrhage four days earlier.

Sutton Wick air crash a Blackburn Beverley of 53 Squadron, Royal Air Forces, crashes into the village of Sutton Wick, Berkshire (now Oxfordshire) killing most of the crew and passengers and two local residents.[9]
Indonesian President Sukarno dismissed the Dewan Perwakilan Rakyat (DPR), 1955 democratically elected parliament, and replaced with DPR-GR, the parliament of his own selected members.
American country music stars Patsy Cline, Hawkshaw Hawkins, Cowboy Copas and their pilot Randy Hughes are killed in a plane crash in Camden, Tennessee.
Aeroflot Flight 191 crashes while landing at Aşgabat International Airport, killing 12.
March Intifada: A Leftist uprising erupts in Bahrain against the British colonial presence.
BOAC Flight 911, a Boeing 707 aircraft, breaks apart in mid-air due to clear-air turbulence and crashes into Mount Fuji, Japan, killing all 124 people on board.[10]
Lake Central Airlines Flight 527 crashes near Marseilles, Ohio, killing 38.[11]
Air France Flight 212 crashes into La Grande Soufrière, killing all 63 aboard.[12]
The Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons goes into effect after ratification by 43 nations.

enAnchors.text: Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons

D0305.en: An Iberia McDonnell Douglas DC-9 collide in mid-air with a Spantax Convair 990 Coronado over Nantes, France, killing all 68 people aboard the DC-9, including music manager Michael Jeffery.[13]
Yom Kippur War: Israeli forces withdraw from the west bank of the Suez Canal.
The Landsat 3 is launched from Vandenberg Air Force Base in California.
Soviet probes Venera 11, Venera 12 and the German-American solar satellite Helios II all are hit by "off the scale" gamma rays leading to the discovery of soft gamma repeaters.
The ZX81, a pioneering British home computer, is launched by Sinclair Research and would go on to sell over 11⁄2 million units around the world.
Soviet probe Venera 14 lands on Venus.
Aeropostal Alas de Venezuela Flight 109 crashes in Venezuela, killing 45.[14]
Palair Macedonian Airlines Flight 301 crashes at Skopje International Airport in Petrovec, North Macedonia, killing 83.[15]
In Mina, Saudi Arabia, 35 pilgrims are killed in a stampede on the Jamaraat Bridge during the Hajj.[16]
An earthquake in Mindanao, Philippines, kills 15 people and injures more than 100.[17]
In Haifa, 17 Israeli civilians are killed in the Haifa bus 37 suicide bombing.
An Antonov An-148 crashes in Russia's Alexeyevsky District, Belgorod Oblast during a test flight, killing all seven aboard.[18]
Tropical Storm Irina kills over 75 as it passes through Madagascar.[19]
Two people are killed and six more are injured in a shooting at a hair salon in Bucharest, Romania.[20]
Syrian civil war: The Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) pause the Deir ez-Zor campaign due to the Turkish-led invasion of Afrin.[21][22][23]
Pope Francis begins a historical visit to Iraq[24] amidst the COVID-19 pandemic.[25]

enAnchors.text: List of pastoral visits of Pope Francis

D0305.en: Twenty people are killed and 30 injured in a suicide car bombing in Mogadishu, Somalia.[26]
The 2023 Estonian parliamentary election is held, with two centre-right liberal parties gaining an absolute majority for the first time.[27]
A group of four prisoners escape from the Nouakchott Civil Prison, before being caught the next day.[28]

D0306.en: The 42 Martyrs of Amorium are killed after refusing to convert to Islam.[2]
The Siege of Château Gaillard ends in a French victory over King John of England, who loses control of Normandy to King Philip II Augustus.[3]
Treaty of Paris of 1323 is signed.[4]
The town of Kajaani, known at the time as Cajanaburg, is founded by Count Per Brahe, the Governor-General of Finland.[5][6]
The First Fleet arrives at Norfolk Island in order to found a convict settlement.[7]

The Missouri Compromise is signed into law by President James Monroe. The compromise allows Missouri to enter the Union as a slave state, brings Maine into the Union as a free state, and makes the rest of the northern part of the Louisiana Purchase territory slavery-free.

Texas Revolution: Battle of the Alamo: After a thirteen-day siege by an army of 3,000 Mexican troops, the 187 Texas volunteers, including frontiersman Davy Crockett and colonel Jim Bowie, defending the Alamo are killed and the fort is captured.
The Supreme Court of the United States rules 7–2 in the Dred Scott v. Sandford case that the Constitution does not confer citizenship on black people.
Anarchist assassin tries to kill German Emperor Wilhelm II.[8]
Scottish National Antarctic Expedition: Led by William Speirs Bruce, the Antarctic region of Coats Land was discovered from the Scotia.[9]
Italo-Turkish War: Italian forces become the first to use airships in war, as two dirigibles drop bombs on Turkish troops encamped at Janzur, from an altitude of 1,800 m.
International Unemployment Day demonstrations globally initiated by the Comintern.
Norman Rockwell published Freedom from Want in The Saturday Evening Post with a matching essay by Carlos Bulosan as part of the Four Freedoms series.
World War II: Generalfeldmarschall Erwin Rommel launches the Battle of Medenine in an attempt to slow down the British Eighth Army. It fails, and he leaves Africa three days later.[10]

World War II: The Battle of Fardykambos, one of the first major battles between the Greek Resistance and the occupying Royal Italian Army, ends with the surrender of an entire Italian battalion, the bulk of the garrison of the town of Grevena, leading to its liberation a fortnight later.[11]
World War II: Soviet Air Forces bomb an evacuated town of Narva in German-occupied Estonia, destroying the entire historical Swedish-era town.
World War II: Cologne is captured by American troops. On the same day, Operation Spring Awakening, the last major German offensive of the war, begins.[12]
Ho Chi Minh signs an agreement with France which recognizes Vietnam as an autonomous state in the Indochinese Federation and the French Union.
Cold War: The trial of Ethel and Julius Rosenberg begins.
Georgy Malenkov succeeds Joseph Stalin as Premier of the Soviet Union and First Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union.
Ghana becomes the first Sub-Saharan country to gain independence from the British.
Nation of Islam leader Elijah Muhammad officially gives boxing champion Cassius Clay the name Muhammad Ali.
Constantine II becomes the last King of Greece.
Premier Tom Playford of South Australia loses power after 27 years in office.
Cold War: Joseph Stalin's daughter Svetlana Alliluyeva defects to the United States.
Three rebels are executed by Rhodesia, the first executions since UDI, prompting international condemnation.
An explosion at the Weather Underground safe house in Greenwich Village kills three.
The Zapruder film of the assassination of John F. Kennedy is shown in motion to a national TV audience for the first time by Robert J. Groden and Dick Gregory.
Algiers Accord: Iran and Iraq announce a settlement of their border dispute.
In the United Kingdom, a walkout at Cortonwood Colliery in Brampton Bierlow signals the start of a strike that lasted almost a year and involved the majority of the country's miners.
The British ferry MS Herald of Free Enterprise capsizes in about 90 seconds, killing 193.[13]
Three Provisional Irish Republican Army volunteers are shot dead by the SAS in Gibraltar in Operation Flavius.
The Michelangelo computer virus begins to affect computers.
Air Algérie Flight 6289 crashes at the Aguenar – Hadj Bey Akhamok Airport in Tamanrasset, Algeria, killing 102 out of the 103 people on board.[14]
A suicide bomber kills 68 people (including first responders) in Baghdad on the same day that a gunman kills eight students in Jerusalem.
Forbes names Jeff Bezos as the world's richest person, for the first time, at $112 billion net worth.[15]
Thirty-two people are killed and 81 are injured when gunmen open fire on a ceremony in Kabul, Afghanistan. The Islamic State claims responsibility for the attack.[16]

D0307.en: Marcus Aurelius and L. Commodus (who changes his name to Lucius Verus) become joint emperors of Rome on the death of Antoninus Pius.[1]
Konrad III von Hohenstaufen was elected king of Germany at Coblenz in the presence of the papal legate Theodwin.[2]
The University of Paris issues the last in a series of condemnations of various philosophical and theological theses.[3]
A peace treaty is signed between the Ottoman Empire and the Republic of Venice, ending the Ottoman–Venetian War and leaving Cyprus in Ottoman hands.[4]
Napoleon Bonaparte captures Jaffa in Palestine and his troops proceed to kill more than 2,000 Albanian captives.[5]
Emperor Napoleon I of France wins the Battle of Craonne.[6]
Shrigley abduction: 15-year old Ellen Turner is abducted by Edward Gibbon Wakefield, a future figure in the establishment of colonies in South Australia and New Zealand.[7][8]
Senator Daniel Webster gives his "Seventh of March" speech endorsing the Compromise of 1850 in order to prevent a possible civil war.[9]
Alexander Graham Bell is granted a patent for an invention he calls the "telephone".[10]
Second Boer War: Boers, led by Koos de la Rey, defeat the British at the Battle of Tweebosch.[11]
The short-lived socialist Labin Republic is proclaimed.[12][13]
The Parliament House of Finland is officially inaugurated in Helsinki, Finland.[14]
Günther Prien and the crew of German submarine U-47, one of the most successful U-boats of World War II, disappear without a trace.[15]
Northwest Orient Airlines Flight 307 crashes in Lynnhurst, Minneapolis, killing 15 people.[16]
Korean War: Operation Ripper: United Nations troops led by General Matthew Ridgway begin an assault against Chinese forces.[17]
Iranian prime minister Ali Razmara is assassinated by Khalil Tahmasebi, a member of the Islamic fundamentalist Fada'iyan-e Islam, outside a mosque in Tehran.[18][19]
Bloody Sunday: A group of 600 civil rights marchers are brutally attacked by state and local police in Selma, Alabama.[20]
Aeroflot Flight 542 crashes in the Yermakovsky District, killing all 31 aboard.[21]
Challenger Disaster: Divers from the USS Preserver locate the crew cabin of Challenger on the ocean floor.[22]
Iran and the United Kingdom break diplomatic relations after a fight over Salman Rushdie and his controversial novel, The Satanic Verses.[23]
The terrorist organisation Lashkar-e-Taiba coordinates a series of bombings in Varanasi, India.[24]
Reform of the House of Lords: The British House of Commons votes to make the upper chamber, the House of Lords, 100% elected.[25]

enAnchors.text: Reform of the House of Lords

D0307.en: Garuda Indonesia Flight 200 crashes at Adisutjipto International Airport in the Special Region of Yogyakarta, Indonesia, killing 21 people.[26]

Massereene Barracks shooting: The Real Irish Republican Army kills two British soldiers and injures two other soldiers and two civilians at Massereene Barracks, the first British military deaths in Northern Ireland since the end of The Troubles.[27][28]
At least 108 die and 615 are injured in the 2021 Bata explosions in Bata, Equatorial Guinea.[29]
Sweden officially joins NATO, becoming its 32nd member.[30]

Hannah Gutierrez-Reed is found guilty of involuntary manslaughter in the death of Halyna Hutchins on the set of the movie Rust, the first time someone has been found guilty for causing a death on a movie set.[31]

D0308.en: Ferdowsi completes his epic poem Shahnameh.[1]
Following the death of his mother, queen Urraca of León, Alfonso VII is proclaimed king of León.[2]

enAnchors.text: Alfonso VII of León and Castile

D0308.en: Battle of Hausbergen between bourgeois militias and the army of the bishop of Strasbourg.[3]
The city of Pori (Swedish: Björneborg) is founded by Duke John on the shores of the Gulf of Bothnia.[4]
Treaty of Roskilde: After a devastating defeat in the Northern Wars (1655–1661), Frederick III, the King of Denmark–Norway is forced to give up nearly half his territory to Sweden.[5]
Queen Anne, the younger sister of Mary II, becomes Queen regnant of England, Scotland, and Ireland.[6]
The Safavid Empire of Iran is defeated by an army from Afghanistan at the Battle of Gulnabad.[7]
Nader Shah, founder of the Afsharid dynasty, is crowned Shah of Iran.[8]

An anonymous writer, thought by some to be Thomas Paine, publishes "African Slavery in America", the first article in the American colonies calling for the emancipation of slaves and the abolition of slavery.[9]

Gnadenhutten massacre: Ninety-six Native Americans in Gnadenhutten, Ohio, who had converted to Christianity, are killed by Pennsylvania militiamen in retaliation for raids carried out by other Indian tribes.[10]

enAnchors.text: Native Americans in the United States

D0308.en: War of the Second Coalition: At the Battle of Abukir, a British force under Sir Ralph Abercromby lands in Egypt with the aim of ending the French campaign in Egypt and Syria.[11]
King Oscar I ascends to the thrones of Sweden and Norway.[12]
The Althing, the parliament of Iceland, was reopened after 45 years of closure.[13]
Sakai incident: Japanese samurai kill 11 French sailors in the port of Sakai, Osaka.[14]
French aviator Raymonde de Laroche becomes the first woman to receive a pilot's license.[15]
World War I: A British force unsuccessfully attempts to relieve the siege of Kut (present-day Iraq) in the Battle of Dujaila.[16]
International Women's Day protests in Petrograd mark the beginning of the February Revolution (February 23 in the Julian calendar).[17]
The United States Senate votes to limit filibusters by adopting the cloture rule.[18]
Spanish Prime Minister Eduardo Dato Iradier is assassinated while on his way home from the parliament building in Madrid.[19]
A mine disaster kills 172 coal miners near Castle Gate, Utah.[20]
Daytona Beach and Road Course holds its first oval stock car race.[21]
Spanish Civil War: The Battle of Guadalajara begins.[22]
World War II: The Dutch East Indies surrender Java to the Imperial Japanese Army.[23]
World War II: Imperial Japanese Army forces captured Rangoon, Burma from British.[24]
The iconic Volkswagen Type 2 "Bus" begins production.[25]
A Turkish Airlines Fokker F27 Friendship crashes into Mount Medetsiz in the Taurus Mountains of Turkey, killing all 11 people on board.[26]
The Ba'ath Party comes to power in Syria in a coup d'état.[27]
Vietnam War: US Marines arrive at Da Nang.[28]
Aeroflot Flight 513 crashes during takeoff from Kuybyshev Airport, killing 30 and injuring 9.[29]
Nelson's Pillar in Dublin, Ireland, is destroyed by a bomb.[30]
Philips demonstrates the compact disc publicly for the first time.[31]
Images taken by Voyager 1 prove the existence of volcanoes on Io, a moon of Jupiter.[32]
Cold War: While addressing a convention of Evangelicals, U.S. President Ronald Reagan labels the Soviet Union an "evil empire".[33]
A supposed failed assassination attempt on Islamic cleric Sayyed Mohammad Hussein Fadlallah in Beirut, Lebanon kills 80 and injures 200 others.[34]
Aeroflot Flight 3379 is hijacked by the Ovechkin family and diverted to Veshchevo in the Soviet Union.[35]
A collision at Indira Gandhi International Airport kills 9 people.[36]
Space Shuttle Discovery launches on STS-102, carrying the Expedition 2 crew to the International Space Station.[37]
A new constitution is signed by Iraq's Governing Council.[38]

Headlined by Hulk Hogan and Ric Flair, TNA Wrestling moved its flagship program, TNA Impact!, to Monday night. This effort to go "big time live" failed but is notable in the history of professional wrestling television.[39]
In one of aviation's greatest mysteries, Malaysia Airlines Flight 370, carrying a total of 239 people, disappears en route from Kuala Lumpur to Beijing.[40] The fate of the flight remains unknown.
The Azure Window, a natural arch on the Maltese island of Gozo, collapses in stormy weather.[41]

The first Aurat March (social/political demonstration) was held being International Women's Day in Karachi, Pakistan, since then annually held  across Pakistan and feminist slogan Mera Jism Meri Marzi (My body, my choice), in demand for women's right to bodily autonomy and against gender-based violence came into vogue in Pakistan.[42][43][44]
International Women's Day marches in Mexico become violent with 62 police officers and 19 civilians injured in Mexico City alone.[45]

Twenty-eight political institutions in Myanmar establish the National Unity Consultative Council, a historic alliance of ethnic armed organizations and democratically elected leaders in response to the 2021 Myanmar coup d'état[46]

D0309.en: First known mention of Lithuania, in the annals of the monastery of Quedlinburg.[2]
Khwarazmian sultan Jalal ad-Din conquers the Georgian capital of Tbilisi.[3]
Bulgarian Tsar Ivan Asen II defeats Theodore of Epirus in the Battle of Klokotnitsa.[4]
The fleet of Pedro Álvares Cabral leaves Lisbon for the Indies. The fleet will discover Brazil which lies within boundaries granted to Portugal in the Treaty of Tordesillas in 1494.[5]
Safavid troops retreat from Basra, ending a three-year occupation.[6]

After a campaign by the writer Voltaire, judges in Paris posthumously exonerate Jean Calas of murdering his son. Calas had been tortured and executed in 1762 on the charge, though his son may have actually died by suicide.[7]
Scottish philosopher Adam Smith publishes The Wealth of Nations, ushering in the classical period of political economy.[8]
Napoléon Bonaparte marries his first wife, Joséphine de Beauharnais.[9]
Paraguayan forces defeat Manuel Belgrano at the Battle of Tacuarí.[10]
Francis Ronalds describes the first battery-operated clock in the Philosophical Magazine.[11]
The U.S. Supreme Court rules in the United States v. The Amistad case that captive Africans who had seized control of the ship carrying them had been taken into slavery illegally.[12]

enAnchors.text: Supreme Court of the United States

D0309.en: Giuseppe Verdi's third opera, Nabucco, receives its première performance in Milan; its success establishes Verdi as one of Italy's foremost opera composers.[13]
The first documented discovery of gold in California occurs at Rancho San Francisco, six years before the California Gold Rush.[14][15]
Mexican–American War: The first large-scale amphibious assault in U.S. history is launched in the Siege of Veracruz.[16]

American Civil War: USS Monitor and CSS Virginia (rebuilt from the engines and lower hull of the USS Merrimack) fight to a draw in the Battle of Hampton Roads, the first battle between two ironclad warships.[17]

Demonstration of 9 March 1883 :  Parisian anarchists, unemployed and carpenters narrowly miss the Presidential palace during a violent protest; first use of the black flag as a symbol of anarchism by Louise Michel.[18]
Inter Milan was founded on Football Club Internazionale, following a schism from A.C. Milan.[19]
Mexican Revolution: Pancho Villa leads nearly 500 Mexican raiders in an attack against the border town of Columbus, New Mexico.[20]
Great Depression: President Franklin D. Roosevelt submits the Emergency Banking Act to Congress, the first of his New Deal policies.[21]
World War II: Dutch East Indies unconditionally surrendered to the Japanese forces in Kalijati, Subang, West Java, and the Japanese completed their Dutch East Indies campaign.[22]
World War II: Soviet Army planes attack Tallinn, Estonia.[23]

enAnchors.text: Bombing of Tallinn in World War II

D0309.en: World War II: A coup d'état by Japanese forces in French Indochina removes the French from power.[24]

enAnchors.text: Japanese coup d'état in French Indochina

D0309.en: World War II: Allied forces carry out firebombing over Tokyo, destroying most of the capital and killing over 100,000 civilians.[25]
Bolton Wanderers stadium disaster at Burnden Park, Bolton, England, kills 33 and injures hundreds more.[26]
McCarthyism: CBS television broadcasts the See It Now episode, "A Report on Senator Joseph McCarthy", produced by Fred Friendly.[27]
Soviet forces suppress mass demonstrations in the Georgian SSR, reacting to Nikita Khrushchev's de-Stalinization policy.[28]
The 8.6 Mw  Andreanof Islands earthquake shakes the Aleutian Islands, causing over $5 million in damage from ground movement and a destructive tsunami.[29]
The Barbie doll makes its debut at the American International Toy Fair in New York.[30]
Dr. Belding Hibbard Scribner implants for the first time a shunt he invented into a patient, which allows the patient to receive hemodialysis on a regular basis.[31]
Sputnik 9 successfully launches, carrying a dog and a human dummy, and demonstrating that the Soviet Union was ready to begin human spaceflight.[32]
Trans World Airlines Flight 553 crashes in a field in Concord Township, Ohio, following a mid-air collision with a Beechcraft Baron, killing 26 people.[33]
The Mars 7 Flyby bus releases the descent module too early, missing Mars.[34]
Forty-two people die in the Cavalese cable car disaster, the deadliest cable car accident in history.[35]
The Hanafi Siege: In a 39-hour standoff, armed Hanafi Muslims seize three Washington, D.C., buildings.[36]
President Soeharto inaugurated Jagorawi Toll Road, the first toll highway in Indonesia, connecting Jakarta, Bogor and Ciawi, West Java.[37]
Chrysler announces its acquisition of American Motors Corporation.[38]
Comet Hale–Bopp: Observers in China, Mongolia and eastern Siberia are treated to a rare double feature as an eclipse permits Hale-Bopp to be seen during the day.[39]
Nupedia, a multi-language online encyclopedia, is launched.[40]
Space Shuttle Discovery makes its final landing after 39 flights.[41]
A truce between the Salvadoran government and gangs in the country goes into effect when 30 gang leaders are transferred to lower security prisons.[42]

Two Eurocopter AS350 Écureuil helicopters collide in mid-air over Villa Castelli, Argentina, killing all 10 people on board both aircraft, including French athletes Florence Arthaud, Camille Muffat and Alexis Vastine, as well as producers and guests for the French TV show Dropped.[43]
Giuseppe Conte, Prime Minister of Italy, announces in a televised address and signs the decree imposing the first nationwide COVID-19 lockdown in the world.[44]
A shooting in the Alsterdorf quarter of Hamburg, Germany, kills eight people and injures another eight.[45]

D0310.en: Roman Emperor Maximian concludes his campaign in North Africa and makes a triumphal entry into Carthage.[2]
The Later Han is founded by Liu Zhiyuan. He declares himself emperor.[3]
After establishing the city of Santo Domingo, Christopher Columbus departs for Spain, leaving his brother in command.[4]
Spaniard Fray Tomás de Berlanga, the fourth Bishop of Panama, discovers the Galápagos Islands by chance on his way to Peru.[5]
Susenyos I defeats the combined armies of Yaqob and Abuna Petros II at the Battle of Gol in Gojjam, making him Emperor of Ethiopia.[6]
Charles I dissolves the Parliament of England, beginning the eleven-year period known as the Personal Rule.[7]
French "Sun King" Louis XIV begins his personal rule of France after the death of his premier, the Cardinal Mazarin.[8]
An agreement between Nader Shah and Russia is signed near Ganja, Azerbaijan and Russian troops are withdrawn from occupied territories.[9]

French Huguenot Jean Calas, who had been wrongly convicted of killing his son, dies after being tortured by authorities; the event inspired Voltaire to begin a campaign for religious tolerance and legal reform.[10]
Emperor Napoleon I is defeated at the Battle of Laon in France.[11]
The Royal Netherlands East Indies Army is created.[12]
The French Foreign Legion is created by Louis Philippe, the King of France, from the foreign regiments of the Kingdom of France.[13]
The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo is ratified by the United States Senate, ending the Mexican–American War.[14]
El Hadj Umar Tall seizes the city of Ségou, destroying the Bamana Empire of Mali.[15]
The first Azerbaijani play, The Adventures of the Vizier of the Khan of Lenkaran, prepared by Akhundov, is performed by Hassan-bey Zardabi and dramatist and Najaf-bey Vezirov.[16][17]
The first successful test of a telephone is made by Alexander Graham Bell.[18]
Almon Strowger patents the Strowger switch, a device which led to the automation of telephone circuit switching.[19]
The Courrières mine disaster, Europe's worst ever, kills 1099 miners in northern France.[20]
By signing the Anglo-Siamese Treaty of 1909, Thailand relinquishes its sovereignty over the Malay states of Kedah, Kelantan, Perlis and Terengganu, which become British protectorates.[21]
Mahatma Gandhi is arrested in India, tried for sedition, and sentenced to six years in prison, only to be released after nearly two years for an appendicitis operation.[22]
The Long Beach earthquake affects the Greater Los Angeles Area, leaving around 108 people dead.[23]
Greek Civil War: The Political Committee of National Liberation is established in Greece by the National Liberation Front.[24]
World War II: The U.S. Army Air Force firebombs Tokyo, and the resulting conflagration kills more than 100,000 people, mostly civilians.[25]
Mildred Gillars ("Axis Sally") is convicted of treason.[26]
Fulgencio Batista leads a successful coup in Cuba.[27]
Tibetan uprising: Fearing an abduction attempt by China, thousands of Tibetans surround the Dalai Lama's palace to prevent his removal.[28]
Military Prime Minister of South Vietnam Nguyễn Cao Kỳ sacks rival General Nguyễn Chánh Thi, precipitating large-scale civil and military dissension in parts of the nation.[29]
In Memphis, Tennessee, James Earl Ray pleads guilty to assassinating Martin Luther King Jr. He later unsuccessfully attempts to recant.[30]
Vietnam War: Captain Ernest Medina is charged by the U.S. military with My Lai war crimes.[31]

John Gorton resigns as Prime Minister of Australia and the leader of the Liberal Party of Australia after a secret ballot vote of confidence, being replaced in both positions by William McMahon.[32][33]
1974 Belgian general election: Elections are held in Belgium for all 212 seats in the Chamber of Representatives, the Belgian Socialist Party taking the majority with 59.[34][35]
Vietnam War: Ho Chi Minh Campaign: North Vietnamese troops attack Ban Mê Thuột in the South on their way to capturing Saigon in the final push for victory over South Vietnam.[36]
Astronomers discover the rings of Uranus.[37]
1979 International Women's Day protests in Tehran: Protestor involvement peaks with 15,000 Iranian women and girls performing a three‐hour-long sit‐in at the Courthouse of Tehran.[38]

enAnchors.text: 1979 International Women's Day protests in Tehran

D0310.en: Syzygy: All nine planets recognized at this time — Mercury to Pluto — align on the same side of the Sun.[39][40]
Air Ontario Flight 1363, a Fokker F-28 Fellowship, crashes at Dryden Regional Airport in Dryden, Ontario, Canada, killing 24.[41]
In Haiti, Prosper Avril is ousted eighteen months after seizing power in a coup d'état in September 1988.[42]
1991 Salvadoran legislative election: The Nationalist Republican Alliance wins 39 of the 84 seats in the Legislative Assembly of El Salvador.[43]
The Dot-com bubble peaks with the NASDAQ Composite stock market index reaching 5,048.62.[44]
The Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter arrives at Mars.[45]
The impeachment of President Park Geun-hye of South Korea in response to a major political scandal is unanimously upheld by the country's Constitutional Court, ending her presidency.[46]

Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302, a Boeing 737 MAX, crashes shortly after take off, killing all 157 passengers and crew. This and the prior Lion Air Flight 610 led to all 387 Boeing 737 MAX aircraft being grounded worldwide.[47]

2022 Hungarian presidential election: The National Assembly of Hungary elects former minister for Family Affairs, Katalin Novák, as president of Hungary in a 137–51 vote, becoming the first female president in the country's history.[48][49]
Silicon Valley Bank collapses due to a run on its deposits, in the second largest bank failure in US history. Its operations are taken over by the FDIC.[50]

2024 Portuguese legislative election: Elections are held in Portugal for all 230 seats in the Assembly of the Republic. The Partido Socialista loses its absolute majority to the Partido Social Democrata, winning 77 and 79 seats respectively.[51][52]

D0311.en: Triumph of Orthodoxy: Empress Theodora II restores the veneration of icons in the Orthodox churches in the Byzantine Empire.[1]
Arnošt of Pardubice becomes the last Bishop of Prague (3 March 1343 O.S.), and, a year later, the first Archbishop of Prague.[2]

enAnchors.text: List of bishops and archbishops of Prague

D0311.en: Battle of Castagnaro: Padua, led by John Hawkwood, is victorious over Giovanni Ordelaffi of Verona.[3]
Guaraní forces living in the Jesuit reductions defeat bandeirantes loyal to the Portuguese Empire at the Battle of Mbororé in present-day Panambí, Argentina.[4]
The Frondeurs and the French government sign the Peace of Rueil.[5]
The Daily Courant, England's first national daily newspaper, is published for the first time.[6]
Queen Anne withholds Royal Assent from the Scottish Militia Bill, the last time a British monarch vetoes legislation.[7]
The signing of the Treaty of Mangalore brings the Second Anglo-Mysore War to an end.[8]
The Battle of Kharda is fought between the Maratha Confederacy and the Nizam of Hyderabad, resulting in Maratha victory.[9]

Flagstaff War: Unhappy with translational differences regarding the Treaty of Waitangi, chiefs Hōne Heke, Kawiti and Māori tribe members chop down the British flagpole for a fourth time and drive settlers out of Kororāreka, New Zealand.[10]
Louis-Hippolyte Lafontaine and Robert Baldwin become the first Prime Ministers of the Province of Canada to be democratically elected under a system of responsible government.[11]
The first performance of Rigoletto by Giuseppe Verdi takes place in Venice.[12]
American Civil War: The Constitution of the Confederate States of America is adopted.[13]
The Great Sheffield Flood kills 238 people in Sheffield, England.[14]
Construction of the Seven Sisters Colliery, South Wales, begins; it is located on one of the richest coal sources in Britain.[15]
Shō Tai formally abdicates his position of King of Ryūkyū, under orders from Tokyo, ending the Ryukyu Kingdom.[16]
The Great Blizzard of 1888 begins along the eastern seaboard of the United States, shutting down commerce and killing more than 400 people.[17]
The Saint-Germain bombing ushers France into the Ère des attentats (1892-1894).[18]
World War I: Mesopotamian campaign: Baghdad falls to Anglo-Indian forces commanded by General Frederick Stanley Maude.[19]
In New York City, Samuel Roxy Rothafel opens the Roxy Theatre.[20]
World War II: United States President Franklin D. Roosevelt signs the Lend-Lease Act into law, allowing American-built war supplies to be shipped to the Allies on loan.[21]
World War II:  The Imperial Japanese Navy attempts a large-scale kamikaze attack on the U.S. Pacific Fleet anchored at Ulithi atoll in Operation Tan No. 2.[22]
World War II: The Empire of Vietnam, a short-lived Japanese puppet state, is established.[23]
Rudolf Höss, the first commandant of Auschwitz concentration camp, is captured by British troops.[24]
The 1977 Hanafi Siege: Around 150 hostages held in Washington, D.C., by Hanafi Muslims are set free after ambassadors from three Islamic nations join negotiations.[25]
Coastal Road massacre: At least 37 are killed and more than 70 are wounded when Fatah hijack an Israeli bus, prompting Israel's Operation Litani.[26]
Hundreds of students protest in the University of Pristina in Kosovo, then part of Yugoslavia, to give their province more political rights. The protests then became a nationwide movement.[27]
Fifteen people are killed when Widerøe Flight 933 crashes into the Barents Sea near Gamvik, Norway.[28]
Bob Hawke is appointed Prime Minister of Australia.[29]
Mikhail Gorbachev is elected to the position of General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union, making Gorbachev the USSR's de facto, and last, head of state.[30]
Lithuania declares independence from the Soviet Union.[31]

enAnchors.text: Act of the Re-Establishment of the State of Lithuania

D0311.en: Patricio Aylwin is sworn in as the first democratically elected President of Chile since 1970.[32]
The International Criminal Court holds its inaugural session in The Hague.[33]
Madrid train bombings: Simultaneous explosions on rush hour trains in Madrid, Spain kill 191 people.[34]
Michelle Bachelet is inaugurated as the first female president of Chile.[35]
Space Shuttle Endeavour launches on STS-123, carrying the first component of the Japanese Kibō module to the International Space Station.[36]
Winnenden school shooting: Fifteen are killed and nine are injured before recent graduate Tim Kretschmer shoots and kills himself, leading to tightened weapons restrictions in Germany.[37]
Economist and businessman Sebastián Piñera is sworn in as President of Chile. Aftershocks of the 2010 Pichilemu earthquakes hit central Chile during the ceremony.[38]

An earthquake measuring 9.0 in magnitude strikes 130 km (81 mi) east of Sendai, Japan, triggering a tsunami killing thousands of people. This event also triggered the second largest nuclear accident in history, and one of only two events to be classified as a Level 7 on the International Nuclear Event Scale.[39]
A U.S. soldier kills 16 civilians in the Panjwayi District of Afghanistan near Kandahar.[40]
A Bombardier Challenger 604 crashes into the Zagros Mountains near the Iranian city of Shar-e-kord, killing all 11 people on board.[41]
The World Health Organization (WHO) declares the COVID-19 virus epidemic a pandemic.[42]
US President Joe Biden signs the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan into law.[43]
The Burmese military kills at least 30 villagers, including 3 Buddhist monks, during the Pinlaung massacre in Shan State, Myanmar.[44]

D0312.en: Vitiges, king of the Ostrogoths ends his siege of Rome and retreats to Ravenna, leaving the city to the victorious Byzantine general, Belisarius.[1]
Election of Urban II as the 159th Pope of the Catholic Church. He is best known for initiating the Crusades.[2]
German city Munich (München) is first mentioned as forum apud Munichen in the Augsburg arbitration by Holy Roman Emperor Friedrich I.[3]
Konrad von Wallenrode is elected the 24th Grand Master of the Teutonic Order (date is O.S.).[4]

enAnchors.text: Grand Master of the Teutonic Order

D0312.en: Start of the Siege of Maastricht, part of the Eighty Years' War.[5]
Ignatius of Loyola and Francis Xavier, founders of the Society of Jesus, are canonized by the Roman Catholic Church.[6]
James II of England landed at Kinsale, starting the Williamite War in Ireland.[7]
Peninsular War: A day after a successful rearguard action, French Marshal Michel Ney once again successfully delays the pursuing Anglo-Portuguese force at the Battle of Redinha.[8]
The Girl Guides (later renamed the Girl Scouts of the USA) are founded in the United States.[9]
The future capital of Australia is officially named Canberra.[10]
Moscow becomes the capital of Russia again after Saint Petersburg held this status for most of the period since 1713.[11]
The Kapp Putsch begins when the Marinebrigade Ehrhardt is ordered to march on Berlin.[12]
In California, the St. Francis Dam fails; the resulting floods kill 431 people.[13]
Mahatma Gandhi begins the Salt March, a 200-mile (320 km) march to the sea to protest the British monopoly on salt in India.[14]
Great Depression: Franklin D. Roosevelt addresses the nation for the first time as President of the United States. This is also the first of his "fireside chats".[9]
Anschluss: German troops occupy and annex Austria.[15]
Winter War: Finland signs the Moscow Peace Treaty with the Soviet Union, ceding almost all of Finnish Karelia.[16]
The most destructive train accident in Finnish history kills 39 and injures 69 people in Turenki, Janakkala.[17]
The Battle of Java ends with the surrender of the American-British-Dutch-Australian Command to the Empire of Japan in Bandung, West Java, Dutch East Indies.[18]
Cold War: The Truman Doctrine is proclaimed to help stem the spread of Communism.[19]
The Llandow air disaster kills 80 people when the aircraft they are travelling in crashes near Sigingstone, Wales. At the time this was the world's deadliest air disaster.[20]
Suharto takes power from Sukarno when the Provisional People's Consultative Assembly inaugurate him as Acting President of Indonesia.[21]
Mauritius gains independence from the United Kingdom.[22]
The 1971 Turkish military memorandum is sent to the Süleyman Demirel government of Turkey and the government resigns.[23]
Tim Berners-Lee submits his proposal to CERN for an information management system, which subsequently develops into the World Wide Web.[24]
Mauritius becomes a republic while remaining a member of the Commonwealth of Nations.[25]
Several bombs explode in Mumbai, India, killing about 300 people and injuring hundreds more.[26]
North Korea announces that it will withdraw from the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons and refuses to allow inspectors access to its nuclear sites.[27]

enAnchors.text: Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons

D0312.en: Former Warsaw Pact members the Czech Republic, Hungary and Poland join NATO.[28]
Zoran Đinđić, Prime Minister of Serbia, is assassinated in Belgrade.[29]
The World Health Organization officially release a global warning of outbreaks of Severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS).[30]
President of South Korea, Roh Moo-hyun, is impeached by its National Assembly, the first such impeachment in the nation's history.[31]
Financier Bernie Madoff pleads guilty to one of the largest frauds in Wall Street's history.[32]
A reactor at the Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Power Plant explodes and releases radioactivity into the atmosphere a day after the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami.[33]
A gas explosion in the New York City neighborhood of East Harlem kills eight and injures 70 others.[34]
US-Bangla Airlines Flight 211 crashes at Tribhuvan International Airport in Katmandu, killing 51 and injuring 20.[35]
In the House of Commons, the revised EU Withdrawal Bill was rejected by a margin of 149 votes.[36]
The United States suspends travel from Europe due to the COVID-19 pandemic.[37]

D0313.en: Roman emperor Elagabalus is murdered alongside his mother, Julia Soaemias. He is replaced by his 14-year old cousin, Severus Alexander.[1][2]
The Battle of Badr, the first major battle between the Muslims and Quraysh.[3]

Siege of Warangal: Sultan Ghiyath al-Din Tughluq sends an expeditionary army led by his son, Muhammad bin Tughluq, to the Kakatiya capital Warangal – after ruler Prataparudra has refused to make tribute payments. He besieges the city and finally, after a campaign of 8 months, Prataparudra surrenders on November 9.[4]
The Battle of Oosterweel, traditionally regarded as the start of the Eighty Years' War.[5]
At the Battle of Tondibi in Mali, Moroccan forces of the Saadi dynasty, led by Judar Pasha, defeat the Songhai Empire, despite being outnumbered by at least five to one.[6]
Harvard College is named after clergyman John Harvard.[7]
Nojpetén, capital of the last independent Maya kingdom, falls to Spanish conquistadors, the final step in the Spanish conquest of Guatemala.[8]
The Battle of Cartagena de Indias (part of the War of Jenkins' Ear) begins.[9]
William Herschel discovers Uranus.[10]
Gustav IV Adolf of Sweden is deposed in the Coup of 1809.[11]
A French and Italian fleet is defeated by a British squadron off the island of Vis in the Adriatic during the Napoleonic Wars.[12]
Participants at the Congress of Vienna declare Napoleon an outlaw following his escape from Elba[13]
Pope Leo XII publishes the apostolic constitution Quo Graviora in which he renewed the prohibition on Catholics joining freemasonry.[14]
Felix Mendelssohn's Violin Concerto receives its première performance in Leipzig with Ferdinand David as soloist.[15]
The German revolutions of 1848–1849 begin in Vienna.[16]
The Act Prohibiting the Return of Slaves is passed by the United States Congress, effectively annulling the Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 and setting the stage for the Emancipation Proclamation.[17]

enAnchors.text: Act Prohibiting the Return of Slaves

D0313.en: The Siege of Khartoum begins. It lasts until January 26, 1885.[18]
The eruption of Ritter Island triggers tsunamis that kill up to 3,000 people on nearby islands.[19]
British forces occupy Bloemfontein, Orange Free State, during the Second Boer War.[20]
The Kapp Putsch briefly ousts the Weimar Republic government from Berlin.[21]
The news of the discovery of Pluto is announced by Lowell Observatory.[22]
The Winter War between Finland and the Soviet Union officially ends after the signing of the Moscow Peace Treaty.[23]
The Holocaust: German forces liquidate the Jewish ghetto in Kraków.[24]

The Battle of Điện Biên Phủ begins with an artillery barrage by Viet Minh forces under Võ Nguyên Giáp; Viet Minh victory led to the end of the First Indochina War and French withdrawal from Vietnam.[25]
Cuban student revolutionaries storm the presidential palace in Havana in a failed attempt on the life of President Fulgencio Batista.[26]
Kitty Genovese is murdered in New York City,[27] prompting research into the bystander effect due to the false story that neighbors witnessed the killing and did nothing to help her.[28]
Apollo 9 returns safely to Earth after testing the Lunar Module.[29]
Sierra Pacific Airlines Flight 802 crashes into the White Mountains near Bishop, California, killing 36.[30]
The New Jewel Movement, headed by Maurice Bishop, ousts the Prime Minister of Grenada, Eric Gairy, in a coup d'état.[31]
The Seikan Tunnel, the longest tunnel in the world with an undersea segment, opens between Aomori and Hakodate, Japan.[32]
Space Shuttle Discovery launches on STS-29 carrying the TDRS-4 satellite.[33]
The Mw  6.6 Erzincan earthquake strikes eastern Turkey with a maximum Mercalli intensity of VIII (Severe).[34]
The 1993 Storm of the Century affects the eastern United States, dropping feet of snow in many areas.[35]
The Dunblane massacre leads to the death of sixteen primary school children and one teacher in Dunblane, Scotland.[36]
The Missionaries of Charity choose Sister Nirmala to succeed Mother Teresa as their leader.[37]
An article in Nature identifies the Ciampate del Diavolo as 350,000-year-old hominid footprints.[38]
The Sierre coach crash kills 28 people, including 22 children.[39]
The 2013 papal conclave elects Cardinal Jorge Mario Bergoglio taking the name Pope Francis as the 266th Pope of the Catholic Church.[40]
The Ankara bombing kills at least 37 people.[41]
Three gunmen attack two hotels in the Ivory Coast town of Grand-Bassam, killing at least 19 people.[42]
President Donald Trump declares the COVID-19 pandemic to be a national emergency in the United States.[43]

enAnchors.text: COVID-19 pandemic in the United States

D0313.en: Breonna Taylor is killed by police officers who were forcibly entering her home in Louisville, Kentucky; her death sparked extensive protests against racism and police brutality.[44]
Katerina Sakellaropoulou is sworn in as the first female President of Greece amid strict COVID-19 measures.[45]

D0314.en: Battle of Mogyoród: Dukes Géza and Ladislaus defeat their cousin Solomon, King of Hungary, forcing him to flee to Hungary's western borderland.[1]
Battle of Ivry: Henry of Navarre and the Huguenots defeat the forces of the Catholic League under Charles, Duke of Mayenne, during the French Wars of Religion.[2]
Thirty Years' War: Bavaria, Cologne, France and Sweden sign the Truce of Ulm.[3]

According to his own account, Otto von Guericke completes his book Experimenta Nova (ut vocantur) Magdeburgica de Vacuo Spatio, detailing his experiments on vacuum and his discovery of electrostatic repulsion.[4]
The Third Anglo-Dutch War: The Battle of Ronas Voe results in the Dutch East India Company ship Wapen van Rotterdam being captured with a death toll of up to 300 Dutch crew and soldiers.[5]
Admiral Sir John Byng is executed by firing squad aboard HMS Monarch for breach of the Articles of War.[6]
American Revolutionary War: Spanish forces capture Fort Charlotte in Mobile, Alabama, the last British frontier post capable of threatening New Orleans.[7]
Eli Whitney is granted a patent for the cotton gin.[8]
Rossini's Petite messe solennelle is first performed, by twelve singers, two pianists and a harmonium player in a mansion in Paris.[9]
The Mikado, a light opera by W. S. Gilbert and Arthur Sullivan, receives its first public performance at the Savoy Theatre in London.[10]
The Gold Standard Act is ratified, placing the United States currency on the gold standard.[11]
Utah governor Heber Manning Wells vetoes a bill that would have eased restriction on polygamy.[12]
Pelican Island National Wildlife Refuge, the first national wildlife refuge in the US, is established by President Theodore Roosevelt.[13]
In the second of the 1920 Schleswig plebiscites, about 80% of the population in Zone II votes to remain part of Weimar Germany.[14]
Six members of a group of Irish Republican Army activists known as the Forgotten Ten, are hanged in Dublin's Mountjoy Prison.[15]
Charlie Daly and three other members of the Irish Republican Army are executed by Irish Free State forces.[16]
The El Virilla train accident, Costa Rica, kills 248 people and wounds another 93 when a train falls off a bridge over the Río Virilla between Heredia and Tibás.[17]
Alam Ara, India's first talking film, is released.[18]
Slovakia declares independence under German pressure.[19]
Anne Miller becomes the first American patient to be treated with penicillin, under the care of Orvan Hess and John Bumstead.[20]
The Holocaust: The liquidation of the Kraków Ghetto is completed.[21]
The R.A.F. drop the Grand Slam bomb in action for the first time, on a railway viaduct near Bielefeld, Germany.[22]
Korean War: United Nations troops recapture Seoul for the second time.[23]
A USAF B-52 bomber carrying nuclear weapons crashes near Yuba City, California.[24]
Jack Ruby is convicted of killing Lee Harvey Oswald, the assassin who had shot and killed John F. Kennedy in the previous year.[25]
The body of U.S. President John F. Kennedy is moved to a permanent burial place at Arlington National Cemetery.[26]
Sterling Airways Flight 296 crashes near Kalba, United Arab Emirates while on approach to Dubai International Airport, killing 112 people.[27]
The Israel Defense Forces launch Operation Litani, a seven-day campaign to invade and occupy southern Lebanon.[28]
Alia Royal Jordanian Flight 600 crashes at Doha International Airport, killing 45 people.[29]
LOT Polish Airlines Flight 007 crashes during final approach near Warsaw, Poland, killing 87 people, including a 14-man American boxing team.[30]
The South African government bombs the headquarters of the African National Congress in London.[31]

enAnchors.text: 1982 bombing of the African National Congress headquarters in London

D0314.en: In the Johnson South Reef Skirmish Chinese forces defeat Vietnamese forces in an altercation over control of one of the Spratly Islands.[32]
Escondida in Chile's Atacama Desert –which was to become the worlds most productive copper mine– is officially inaugurated.[33]
Norman Thagard becomes the first American astronaut to ride to space on board a Russian launch vehicle.[34]
The 2006 Chadian coup d'état attempt ends in failure.[35]
Operation Bringing Home the Goods: Israeli troops raid an American-supervised Palestinian prison in Jericho to capture six Palestinian prisoners, including PFLP chief Ahmad Sa'adat.[36]
The Nandigram violence in Nandigram, West Bengal, results in the deaths of at least 14 people.[37]
A series of riots, protests, and demonstrations erupt in Lhasa and subsequently spread elsewhere in Tibet.[38]
A naming ceremony for the chemical element nihonium takes place in Tokyo, with then Crown Prince Naruhito in attendance.[39][40]
Cyclone Idai makes landfall near Beira, Mozambique, causing devastating floods and over 1,000 deaths.[41]
Burmese security forces kill at least 65 civilians in the Hlaingthaya massacre.[42]

D0315.en: Odoacer, the first barbarian King of Italy after the fall of the Western Roman Empire, is slain by Theoderic the Great, king of the Ostrogoths, while the two kings were feasting together.[3]
Michael III, emperor of the Byzantine Empire, overthrows the regency of his mother, empress Theodora (wife of Theophilos) with support of the Byzantine nobility.[4]
Al-Hadi ila'l-Haqq Yahya enters Sa'dah and founds the Zaydi Imamate of Yemen.[5]
After a ten-year truce, German King Henry the Fowler defeats a Hungarian army at the Battle of Riade near the Unstrut river.[6]
Battle of Halmyros: The Catalan Company defeats Walter V, Count of Brienne to take control of the Duchy of Athens, a Crusader state in Greece.[7]
Mughal Emperor Akbar abolishes the jizya tax on non-Muslim subjects.[8]
a dam failure causes the sudden flooding of the mining city of Potosí in present-day Bolivia leading to the death of thousands and the massive release of toxic mercury into the environment.[9][10]
King Charles II of England issues the Royal Declaration of Indulgence, granting limited religious freedom to all Christians.[11]
In an emotional speech in Newburgh, New York, George Washington asks his officers not to support the Newburgh Conspiracy. The plea is successful, and the threatened coup d'état never takes place.[12]
Maine is admitted as the twenty-third U.S. state.[13]
Sailor Benjamin Morrell erroneously reported the existence of the island of New South Greenland near Antarctica.[14]
A revolution breaks out in Hungary, and the Habsburg rulers are compelled to meet the demands of the reform party.[15]
France and Vietnam sign the Second Treaty of Saigon, further recognizing the full sovereignty of France over Cochinchina.[16]
Archbishop of New York John McCloskey is named the first cardinal in the United States.[17]
First ever official cricket test match is played: Australia vs England at the MCG Stadium, in Melbourne, Australia.[18]

enAnchors.text: First ever official cricket test match is played

D0315.en: Start of the Anglo-Tibetan War of 1888.[19]
The Lobau bombing is one of the first attacks of the Ère des attentats (1892-1894).[20]
Madeleine bombing by Désiré Pauwels during the Ère des attentats.[21]
The first parliamentary elections of Finland (at the time the Grand Duchy of Finland) are held.[22]
Tsar Nicholas II of Russia abdicates the Russian throne, ending the 304-year Romanov dynasty.[23][24]
Finnish Civil War: The battle of Tampere begins.[25]
Ukrainian War of Independence: The Kontrrazvedka is established as the counterintelligence division of the Revolutionary Insurgent Army of Ukraine.[26]
The American Legion is founded.
Talaat Pasha, former Grand Vizir of the Ottoman Empire and chief architect of the Armenian genocide is assassinated in Berlin by a 23-year-old Armenian, Soghomon Tehlirian.[27]
After Egypt gains nominal independence from the United Kingdom, Fuad I becomes King of Egypt.[28]
The first Women's Boat Race between the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge takes place on The Isis in Oxford.[29]
Germany occupies Czechoslovakia.[30]
Carpatho-Ukraine declares itself an independent republic,[31] but is annexed by Hungary the next day.[32]
World War II: Third Battle of Kharkiv: The Germans retake the city of Kharkiv from the Soviet armies.[33]
Iranian oil industry is nationalized.[34]

enAnchors.text: Nationalization of the Iranian oil industry

D0315.en: At the 1961 Commonwealth Prime Ministers' Conference, South Africa announces that it will withdraw from the Commonwealth when the South African Constitution of 1961 comes into effect.[35]
President Lyndon B. Johnson, responding to the Selma crisis, tells U.S. Congress "We shall overcome" while advocating the Voting Rights Act.[36]
Fifteen people are killed when Sterling Airways Flight 901, a Sud Aviation Caravelle, catches fire following a landing gear collapse at Mehrabad International Airport in Tehran, Iran.[37]
Somalia and Ethiopia signed a truce to end the Ethio-Somali War.[38]
Collapse of Hotel New World: Thirty-three people die when the Hotel New World in Singapore collapses.[39]
Mikhail Gorbachev is elected as the first President of the Soviet Union.[40]
Cold War: The Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany comes into effect, granting full sovereignty to the Federal Republic of Germany.[41]

enAnchors.text: Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany

D0315.en: Stockpiles of obsolete ammunition explode at an ex-military ammunition depot in the village of Gërdec, Albania, killing 26 people.[42]
Beginning of the Syrian revolution.[43]
Fifty-one people are killed in the Christchurch mosque shootings.[44]
Beginning of the 2019–20 Hong Kong protests.[45]
Approximately 1.4 million young people in 123 countries go on strike to protest climate change.[46]
The 2022 Sri Lankan protests begins amidst Sri Lanka's economic collapse.[47]

D0316.en: Massacre of Jews at Clifford's Tower, York.[1]
Over 200 Cathars who refuse to recant are burnt to death after the Fall of Montségur.[2]
Amidst the Red Turban Rebellions, Han Lin'er, a claimed descendant of Emperor Huizong of Song, is proclaimed emperor of the restored Song dynasty in Bozhou.[3]
Samoset, an Abenaki, visits the settlers of Plymouth Colony and greets them, "Welcome, Englishmen! My name is Samoset.
The Long Parliament of England is dissolved so as to prepare for the new Convention Parliament.[4]
The Dutch bombard Givet during the Nine Years' War.[5]
King Gustav III of Sweden is shot; he dies on March 29.[6]
The Army Corps of Engineers is established to found and operate the United States Military Academy at West Point.

enAnchors.text: United States Army Corps of Engineers

D0316.en: Prince Willem proclaims himself King of the United Kingdom of the Netherlands, the first constitutional monarch in the Netherlands.
The Wanderers F.C. win the first FA Cup, the oldest football competition in the world, beating Royal Engineers A.F.C. 1–0 at The Oval in Kennington, London.
In Melbourne, the representatives of five colonies adopt a constitution, which would become the basis of the Commonwealth of Australia.[7][page needed]
The 7th and 10th US cavalry regiments under John J. Pershing cross the US–Mexico border to join the hunt for Pancho Villa.
Finnish Civil War: Battle of Länkipohja is infamous for its bloody aftermath as the Whites execute 70–100 capitulated Reds.
In accordance with the Treaty of Rome, Fiume becomes annexed as part of Italy.
An earthquake (measuring around 7.0 magnitude) occurs in Dali, China, killing an estimated 5,000 people.[8]
History of Rocketry: Robert Goddard launches the first liquid-fueled rocket, at Auburn, Massachusetts.[9]
Adolf Hitler orders Germany to rearm itself in violation of the Treaty of Versailles. Conscription is reintroduced to form the Wehrmacht.
Warmer-than-normal temperatures rapidly melt snow and ice on the upper Allegheny and Monongahela rivers, leading to a major flood in Pittsburgh.
From Prague Castle, Hitler proclaims Bohemia and Moravia a German protectorate.
Operation Appearance takes place to re-establish British Somaliland[10]
World War II: The Battle of Iwo Jima ends, but small pockets of Japanese resistance persist.
Ninety percent of Würzburg, Germany is destroyed in only 20 minutes by British bombers, resulting in at least 4,000 deaths.[11]

enAnchors.text: Bombing of Würzburg in World War II

D0316.en: Flying Tiger Line Flight 739 disappears in the western Pacific Ocean with all 107 aboard missing and presumed dead.[12][13]
Launch of Gemini 8 with astronauts Neil Armstrong and David Scott. It would perform the first docking of two spacecraft in orbit.[14]
Vietnam War: My Lai massacre occurs; between 347 and 500 Vietnamese villagers are killed by American troops.[15]
A Viasa McDonnell Douglas DC-9 crashes in Maracaibo, Venezuela, killing 155.[16][17]
Assassination of Kamal Jumblatt, the main leader of the anti-government forces in the Lebanese Civil War.
Former Italian Prime Minister Aldo Moro is kidnapped; he is later murdered by his captors.[18]
A Balkan Bulgarian Airlines Tupolev Tu-134 crashes near Gabare, Bulgaria, killing 73.[19]
Supertanker Amoco Cadiz splits in two after running aground on the Portsall Rocks, three miles off the coast of Brittany, resulting in the largest oil spill in history at that time.[20]
Sino-Vietnamese War: The People's Liberation Army crosses the border back into China, ending the war.[21]
William Buckley, the CIA station chief in Lebanon, is kidnapped by Hezbollah; he later dies in captivity.[22]
Associated Press newsman Terry Anderson is taken hostage in Beirut; he is not released until December 1991.[23]
Iran–Contra affair: Lieutenant Colonel Oliver North and Vice Admiral John Poindexter are indicted on charges of conspiracy to defraud the United States.[24]

Halabja chemical attack: The Kurdish town of Halabja in Iraq is attacked with a mix of poison gas and nerve agents on the orders of Saddam Hussein, killing 5,000 people and injuring about 10,000 people.[25]

The Troubles: Ulster loyalist militant Michael Stone attacks a Provisional IRA funeral in Belfast with pistols and grenades. Three persons, one of them a member of PIRA are killed, and more than 60 others are wounded.

Mississippi formally ratifies the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, becoming the last state to approve the abolition of slavery.[26] The Thirteenth Amendment was officially ratified in 1865.

enAnchors.text: Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution

D0316.en: A series of bomb blasts in the city of Shijiazhuang, China kill 108 people and injure 38 others,[27] the biggest mass murder in China in decades.[28]
American activist Rachel Corrie is killed in Rafah by being run over by an Israel Defense Forces bulldozer while trying to obstruct the demolition of a home.[29]
Israel officially hands over Jericho to Palestinian control.[30]
The Kasubi Tombs, Uganda's only cultural World Heritage Site, are destroyed in a fire.[31]
Former Indian cricketer Sachin Tendulkar becomes the first batter in history to score 100 centuries in international cricket.[32]

enAnchors.text: List of international cricket centuries by Sachin Tendulkar

D0316.en: Crimea votes in a controversial referendum to secede from Ukraine to join Russia.[33]
A bomb detonates in a bus carrying government employees in Peshawar, Pakistan, killing 15 and injuring at least 30.[34]
Two suicide bombers detonate their explosives at a mosque during morning prayer on the outskirts of Maiduguri, Nigeria, killing 24 and injuring 18.[35]

The Dow Jones Industrial Average falls by 2,997.10, the single largest point drop in history and the second-largest percentage drop ever at 12.93%, an even greater crash than Black Monday (1929). This follows the U.S. Federal Reserve announcing that it will cut its target interest rate to 0–0.25%.[36]

enAnchors.text: List of largest daily changes in the Dow Jones Industrial Average

D0316.en: Atlanta spa shootings: Eight people are killed and one is injured in a trio of shootings at spas in and near Atlanta, Georgia, U.S. A suspect is arrested the same day.[37]
A 7.4-magnitude earthquake occurs off the coast of Fukushima, Japan, killing 4 people and injuring 225.[38]
Mariupol theatre airstrike during the siege of Mariupol.[39]
A fire breaks out in a nightclub in Kočani, North Macedonia, killing at least 59 people and injuring 155 others.[40]

D0317.en: Commodus becomes sole emperor of the Roman Empire at the age of eighteen, following the death of his father, Marcus Aurelius.[1]
Petronius Maximus becomes, with support of the Roman Senate, emperor of the Western Roman Empire; he forces Licinia Eudoxia, the widow of his predecessor, Valentinian III, to marry him.[2]
Edward, the Black Prince is made Duke of Cornwall, the first Duchy in England.
Turko-Mongol emperor Timur sacks Damascus.[3]
American Revolution: The British Army evacuates Boston, ending the Siege of Boston, after George Washington and Henry Knox place artillery in positions overlooking the city.
The Italian Republic, with Napoleon as president, becomes the Kingdom of Italy, with Napoleon as King of Italy.

The Anglo-Dutch Treaty is signed in London, dividing the Malay archipelago. As a result, the Malay Peninsula is dominated by the British, while Sumatra and Java and surrounding areas are dominated by the Dutch.
The Female Relief Society of Nauvoo is formally organized with Emma Smith as president.[4]
The First Taranaki War begins in Taranaki, New Zealand, a major phase of the New Zealand Wars.
The Kingdom of Italy is proclaimed.
The first railway line of Finland between cities of Helsinki and Hämeenlinna, called Päärata, is officially opened.[5][6]
SS Utopia collides with HMS Anson in the Bay of Gibraltar and sinks, killing 562 of the 880 passengers on board.[7]
The Second Polish Republic adopts the March Constitution.
Holocaust: The first Jews from the Lvov Ghetto are gassed at the Belzec death camp in what is today eastern Poland.
The Ludendorff Bridge in Remagen, Germany, collapses, ten days after its capture.
Belgium, France, Luxembourg, the Netherlands and the United Kingdom sign the Treaty of Brussels, a precursor to the North Atlantic Treaty establishing NATO.[8]
Researchers at the University of California, Berkeley announce the creation of element 98, which they name "californium".[9]
A plane crash in Cebu, Philippines kills Philippine President Ramon Magsaysay and 24 others.
The United States launches the first solar-powered satellite, which is also the first satellite to achieve a long-term orbit.[10]
U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower signs the National Security Council directive on the anti-Cuban covert action program that will ultimately lead to the Bay of Pigs Invasion.
Northwest Orient Airlines Flight 710 crashes in Tobin Township, Perry County, Indiana, killing 63.[11]
Mount Agung erupts on Bali killing more than 1,100 people.
Off the coast of Spain in the Mediterranean, the DSV Alvin submarine finds a missing American hydrogen bomb.
As a result of nerve gas testing by the U.S. Army Chemical Corps in Skull Valley, Utah, over 6,000 sheep are found dead.
Golda Meir becomes the first female Prime Minister of Israel.

The Pulitzer Prize–winning photograph Burst of Joy is taken, depicting a former prisoner of war being reunited with his family, which came to symbolize the end of United States involvement in the Vietnam War.
The Penmanshiel Tunnel collapses during engineering works, killing two workers.
Serial killer Richard Ramirez, aka the "Night Stalker", commits the first two murders in his Los Angeles murder spree.
A Colombian Boeing 727 jetliner, Avianca Flight 410, crashes into a mountainside near the Venezuelan border killing 143.

Eritrean War of Independence: The Nadew Command, an Ethiopian army corps in Eritrea, is attacked on three sides by military units of the Eritrean People's Liberation Front in the opening action of the Battle of Afabet.
Israeli Embassy attack in Buenos Aires: Car bomb attack kills 29 and injures 242.

enAnchors.text: 1992 attack on Israeli embassy in Buenos Aires

D0317.en: A referendum to end apartheid in South Africa is passed 68.7% to 31.2%.

Five hundred and thirty members of the Ugandan cult Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments of God die in a fire, considered to be a mass murder or suicide orchestrated by leaders of the cult. Elsewhere another 248 members are later found dead.

enAnchors.text: Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments of God

D0317.en: Leader of the House of Commons and Lord President of the Council, Robin Cook, resigns from the British Cabinet in disagreement with government plans for the 2003 invasion of Iraq.

enAnchors.text: Leader of the House of Commons

D0317.en: Unrest in Kosovo: More than 22 are killed and 200 wounded. Thirty-five Serbian Orthodox shrines in Kosovo and two mosques in Serbia are destroyed.
Rojava conflict: At a conference in Rmelan, the Movement for a Democratic Society declares the establishment of the Democratic Federation of Northern Syria.[12]

D0318.en: Roman Senate annuls Tiberius' will and proclaims Gaius Julius Caesar Augustus Germanicus (aka Caligula = Little Boots) emperor.[1]
An earthquake in the Levant and the Arabian Peninsula leaves up to 20,000 dead.
Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor, declares himself King of Jerusalem in the Sixth Crusade.
First Mongol invasion of Poland: Mongols overwhelm Polish armies in Kraków in the Battle of Chmielnik and plunder the city.
Jacques de Molay, the 23rd and final Grand Master of the Knights Templar, is burned at the stake.
Albert II of Habsburg becomes King of the Romans.
Valletta is made the capital city of Malta.[2]
Susenyos is formally crowned Emperor of Ethiopia.
The Third Anglo-Powhatan War begins in the Colony of Virginia.
English lord John Berkeley sold his half of New Jersey to the Quakers[3]

enAnchors.text: John Berkeley, 1st Baron Berkeley of Stratton

D0318.en: New York governor George Clarke's complex at Fort George is burned in an arson attack, starting the New York Conspiracy of 1741.
American Revolution: The British Parliament repeals the Stamp Act.
The first modern republic in Germany, the Republic of Mainz, is declared by Andreas Joseph Hofmann.
Flanders Campaign of the French Revolution, Battle of Neerwinden.

enAnchors.text: Low Countries theatre of the War of the First Coalition

D0318.en: Six farm labourers from Tolpuddle, Dorset, England are sentenced to be transported to Australia for forming a trade union.
The premiere of Fry's Leonora in Philadelphia is the first known performance of a grand opera by an American composer.[4]
Revolutions of 1848: A rebellion arose in Milan which in five days of street fighting drove Marshal Radetzky and his Austrian soldiers from the city.[5]
American Civil War: The Congress of the Confederate States adjourns for the last time.
Declaration of the Paris Commune; President of the French Republic, Adolphe Thiers, orders the evacuation of Paris.
The Hawaiian Kingdom signs a treaty with the United States granting exclusive trade rights.
Phoebe, a satellite of Saturn, becomes the first to be discovered with photographs, taken in August 1898, by William Henry Pickering.[6]
The Kumasi Mutiny of 1901 begins.[7]
Macario Sakay issues Presidential Order No. 1 of his Tagalog Republic.[8]
King George I of Greece is assassinated in the recently liberated city of Thessaloniki.
World War I: During the Battle of Gallipoli, three battleships are sunk during a failed British and French naval attack on the Dardanelles.
The second Peace of Riga is signed between Poland and the Soviet Union.
The Kronstadt rebellion is suppressed by the Red Army.[9]

Mongolian Revolution of 1921: The Mongolian People's Army defeated local Chinese forces at Altanbulag, Selenge (then known as Maimachen). This battle was seen as the birthday of the People's Army and completed the expulsion of Chinese militants in Mongolia.[10]
In India, Mohandas Gandhi is sentenced to six years in prison for civil disobedience, of which he serves only two.
The 1925 Tri-State tornado hits the Midwestern states of Missouri, Illinois, and Indiana, killing 695 people.
The New London School explosion in New London, Texas, kills 300 people, mostly children.
Spanish Civil War: Spanish Republican forces defeat the Italians at the Battle of Guadalajara.
Mexico creates Pemex by expropriating all foreign-owned oil reserves and facilities.
World War II: Adolf Hitler and Benito Mussolini meet at the Brenner Pass in the Alps and agree to form an alliance against France and the United Kingdom.
The War Relocation Authority is established in the United States to take Japanese Americans into custody.
Mount Vesuvius in Italy erupts, killing 26 people, causing thousands to flee their homes, and destroying dozens of Allied bombers.

40th Infantry Division, spearheaded by the 185th US Infantry Regiment, landed unopposed in Tigbauan forcing the Japanese forces to surrender and General Macario Peralta and Gen. Gen. Eichelberger to declare the Liberation of Panay, Romblon and Guimaras.[11]
Soviet consultants leave Yugoslavia in the first sign of the Tito–Stalin split.
An earthquake hits western Turkey, killing at least 1,070 people.
The Hawaii Admission Act is signed into law.
The Évian Accords end the Algerian War of Independence, which had begun in 1954.
Cosmonaut Alexei Leonov, leaving his spacecraft Voskhod 2 for 12 minutes, becomes the first person to walk in space.
United Arab Airlines Flight 749 crashes on approach to Cairo International Airport in Cairo, Egypt, killing 30 people.[12]
The supertanker Torrey Canyon runs aground off the Cornish coast.
Gold standard: The U.S. Congress repeals the requirement for a gold reserve to back US currency.
The United States begins secretly bombing the Sihanouk Trail in Cambodia, used by communist forces to infiltrate South Vietnam.
Lon Nol ousts Prince Norodom Sihanouk of Cambodia.
Peru: A landslide crashes into Yanawayin Lake, killing 200 people at the mining camp of Chungar.
Güzel İstanbul, a nude sculpture by Gürdal Duyar in Istanbul is torn down in the middle of the night.[13]
A Vostok-2M rocket at Plesetsk Cosmodrome Site 43 explodes during a fueling operation, killing 48 people.
Germans in the German Democratic Republic vote in the first democratic elections in the former communist dictatorship.
In the largest art theft in US history, 12 paintings, collectively worth around $500 million, are stolen from the Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum in Boston.[14]

Bosnia's Bosniaks and Croats sign the Washington Agreement, ending war between the Croatian Republic of Herzeg-Bosnia and the Republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina, and establishing the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina.
A nightclub fire in Quezon City, Philippines kills 162 people.
The tail of a Russian Antonov An-24 charter plane breaks off while en route to Turkey, causing the plane to crash and killing all 50 people on board.
The parliaments of Russia and Crimea sign an accession treaty.

enAnchors.text: Annexation of Crimea by the Russian Federation

D0318.en: The Bardo National Museum in Tunisia is attacked by gunmen. Twenty-four people, almost all tourists, are killed, and at least 50 other people are wounded.
Israel launches widespread aerial bombardments and attacks on the Gaza Strip, killing at least 591 people, including children.[15]

enAnchors.text: March 2025 Israeli attacks on the Gaza Strip

D0319.en: The Byzantine–Venetian treaty of 1277 is concluded, stipulating a two-year truce and renewing Venetian commercial privileges in the Byzantine Empire.[1]
A Mongol victory at the Battle of Yamen ends the Song dynasty in China.
The Statute of Rhuddlan incorporates the Principality of Wales into England.[2]
Frederick III of Habsburg is the last Holy Roman Emperor crowned by medieval tradition in Rome by Pope Nicholas V.[3]
The Edict of Amboise is signed, ending the first phase of the French Wars of Religion and granting certain freedoms to the Huguenots.
The House of Commons of England passes an act abolishing the House of Lords, declaring it "useless and dangerous to the people of England".

enAnchors.text: History of reform of the House of Lords

D0319.en: Explorer Robert Cavelier de La Salle, searching for the mouth of the Mississippi River, is murdered by his own men.
Charles IV, king of Spain, abdicates after riots and a popular revolt at the winter palace Aranjuez. His son, Ferdinand VII, takes the throne.[4]
The Cortes of Cádiz promulgates the Spanish Constitution of 1812.
American explorer Benjamin Morrell departed Antarctica after a voyage later plagued by claims of fraud.[5]
First documented bank heist in U.S. history, when burglars stole $245,000 (1831 values) from the City Bank (now Citibank) on Wall Street.[6] Most of the money was recovered.

enAnchors.text: 1831 City Bank of New York theft

D0319.en: American Civil War: The Battle of Bentonville begins. By the end of the battle two days later, Confederate forces had retreated from Four Oaks, North Carolina.
Louis Riel declares a provisional government in Saskatchewan, beginning the North-West Rebellion.
The US Congress establishes time zones and approves daylight saving time.
The United States Senate rejects the Treaty of Versailles for the second time (the first time was on November 19, 1919).

Irish War of Independence: One of the biggest engagements of the war takes place at Crossbarry, County Cork. About 100 Irish Republican Army (IRA) volunteers escape an attempt by over 1,300 British forces to encircle them.
Governor Fred B. Balzar signs a bill legalizing gambling in Nevada.
The Sydney Harbour Bridge is opened.[7]
Frank Nitti, the Chicago Outfit Boss after Al Capone, commits suicide at the Chicago Central Railyard.
World War II: The German army occupies Hungary.
World War II: Off the coast of Japan, a dive bomber hits the aircraft carrier USS Franklin, killing 724 of her crew. Badly damaged, the ship is able to return to the US under her own power.
World War II: Adolf Hitler issues his "Nero Decree" ordering all industries, military installations, shops, transportation facilities, and communications facilities in Germany to be destroyed.
French Guiana, Guadeloupe, Martinique, and Réunion become overseas départements of France.
The Monarch Underwear Company fire leaves 24 dead and 15 injured.
The Algerian War of Independence ends.
Over 500,000 Brazilians attend the March of the Family with God for Liberty, in protest against the government of João Goulart and against communism.

enAnchors.text: March of the Family with God for Liberty

D0319.en: The wreck of the SS Georgiana, valued at over $50,000,000 and said to have been the most powerful Confederate cruiser, is discovered by teenage diver and pioneer underwater archaeologist E. Lee Spence, exactly 102 years after its destruction.
The 385-metre-tall (1,263 ft) TV-mast at Emley Moor transmitting station, United Kingdom, collapses due to ice build-up.
The United States House of Representatives begins broadcasting its day-to-day business via the cable television network C-SPAN.
